messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
Why stealing is an aspect of law?

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks why stealing is an aspect of law. This requires a contrast between the law system (which defines, prohibits, and punishes theft) and the grace system (where theft is not even a concept because the believer is in a state of having all needs supplied in Christ). I will draw from the "Collection of Studies" document which contrasts law and grace allegorically, particularly the contrast between "Killer & Life Giver" (2Cor 3:6) and "Corruption & Non-corruption" (Acts 13:36,37). The "God of all grace" document establishes that God is not both law and grace, but all grace—meaning law's operations (like defining theft) are not of God. The "law not of faith frustrations" document reinforces that law produces frustration, not faith. I will also use the "law worketh wrath" connections from the Collection document (Rom 4:15, Col 3:8, Eph 4:31, 1Thess 5:9) to show that stealing is a byproduct of the law system itself. The key insight is that where there is no law, sin (including theft) is not imputed (Rom 4:15; 5:13). The law creates the very transgression it condemns. I will structure the response around: (1) the law defines and thus creates theft as a category, (2) the law's operation produces the desire to steal through prohibition, (3) grace removes the entire framework of theft by placing the believer in a state of fullness in Christ. I will use multiple footnotes from different documents to support each point.

Stealing is an aspect of law because the law system operates by defining, prohibiting, and then punishing transgression—it creates the very category of "theft" that it then condemns. The apostle Paul makes this crystalline in Romans 4:15: "because the law worketh wrath: for where no law is, there is no transgression" [^1]. The law does not prevent stealing; rather, it defines what stealing is, imputes it as sin, and then produces wrath (the penalty) upon the thief. This is the cOLD covenant mechanism: the law gives strength to sin (1Cor 15:56), meaning the very prohibition "Thou shalt not steal" stirs up the desire to steal in the fallen flesh. The law is a killer, not a life-giver, as 2Corinthians 3:6 contrasts: "the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life" [^2]. The law's letter says "do not steal" and then kills you when you do—this is its inherent nature.

The deeper mystery is that the law itself is the source of the problem it claims to solve. Romans 7:8 explains: "But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence. For without the law sin was dead" [^3]. The commandment "Thou shalt not covet" (which includes stealing) actually produced the very coveting it forbade. This is why Paul elsewhere connects law directly to wrath: "Law worketh WRATH" (Rom 4:15), "Put off WRATH (aka law)" (Col 3:8), "Put away WRATH (aka law)" (Eph 4:31), "God didn't appoint us to WRATH (aka law)" (1Thess 5:9) [^4]. The law system is a closed loop of prohibition → transgression → wrath. Stealing is not an aspect of grace; it is an aspect of law because only under law is there a commandment against it, only under law is there imputation of sin, and only under law is there punishment. In grace, there is no law to transgress, no theft to impute.

Grace removes the entire framework. Romans 6:14 declares: "For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace" [^5]. When the believer is not "under" law but "with" grace, the concept of stealing dissolves because the believer is in Christ, where all needs are supplied (Phil 4:19) and where the old man who stole has been crucified with Christ (Rom 6:6). The "God of all grace" is not law, nor both law and grace, but all grace [^6]. This means grace has no prohibition against stealing because grace does not operate by prohibition—it operates by transformation. The one who stole now has the mind of Christ and the fullness of God's provision. The law says "stop stealing or be punished"; grace says "you are a new creation in Christ, and stealing is not even in your vocabulary because you have all things in Him." This is why the allegoric contrasts show law as "Killer" and grace as "Life Giver" [^2]—the law kills the thief; grace makes the thief a son who has no need to steal.

Learn More:
- [Collection of Studies containing mystery and contrast law vs grace](/pages/collection_of_studies_containing_mystery_and_contrast_law_vs_grace)
- [God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace](/pages/god_of_all_grace_is_not_law_nor_both_law_&_grace_but_all_grace)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: Collection of Studies containing mystery and contrast law vs grace
1.snippet: Rom 4:15 ... Col 3:8 ... Eph 4:31 ... 1Thess 5:9 Allegory tells us (of them/us) to put away law. Romans 4:15 says Law worketh WRATH. Colossians 3:8 says Put off WRATH (aka law). Ephesians 4:31 says Put away WRATH (aka law). 1Thessalonians 5:9 says God didn't appoint us to WRATH (aka law).

2.title: Collection of Studies containing mystery and contrast law vs grace
2.snippet: Contrasted as Killer & Life Giver in 2Corinthians 3:6.

3.title: God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace
3.snippet: For law, by being both good & 'evil' is lastly reverse of 'live'. So to think law (wrath) is of God is to think death is of God. But where no law, there no transgression: Rom 4:15, [[[and]]] sin is not imputed when there is no law: Rom 4:8; 5:13.

4.title: Collection of Studies containing mystery and contrast law vs grace
4.snippet: Bible Mystery: Connecting verses about WRATH. Rom 4:15 ... Col 3:8 ... Eph 4:31 ... 1Thess 5:9. Allegory tells us (of them/us) to put away law.

5.title: God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace
5.snippet: For we are not under the law, but under grace: Rom 6:14.

6.title: God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace
6.snippet: 'God of all grace' is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace. Reasoning: God isn't both Law & Grace, but Law or Grace. Why? Both would be a bi-polar oxy-moron: 2 contrary things that can't co-exist in peace.
